context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m on Problem Set 4 for 8.03SC, and i’m kinda stuck on the beaded string one rn. can we start with 4.1, esp the boundary condition idea with the walls?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's dig into 4.1! Before we get into the boundary conditions, tell me: what does a fixed wall actually *do* to the string physically? What constraint does it impose on the beads near it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on Problem 4.1. I should assess where they are before providing any scaffolding. I'll ask them what they already know or have tried, using a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the physics of the wall boundary condition before guiding them further.

exchanges.student: i think the displacement at the wall has to stay 0 always, since it’s fixed. i’m just not fully seeing how that turns into \(A_1=-A_0\).
exchanges.tutor: Good — zero displacement at the wall is exactly right. Now, the problem says to extend to an *infinite* system and introduce an imaginary bead A0 just outside the left wall. If the wall sits exactly halfway between bead 0 and bead 1, what value would A0 need to take so that the displacement at the wall's position is zero?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the zero-displacement condition at the wall. Now I should guide them to connect that physical condition to the idea of extending to an infinite system with an imaginary bead A0. The key insight is that if you place an imaginary bead at a mirror position, what value must it have to enforce zero displacement at the wall? I'll ask a targeted Socratic question about the geometry of the extension.

exchanges.student: ohh if the wall is halfway between them, then the midpoint displacement should be 0, so \((A_0+A_1)/2=0\). so that gives \(A_0=-A_1\), right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that's the boundary condition for the left wall. Now can you apply the same reasoning to the right-hand wall for part (b)? What imaginary bead would you introduce, and where does it sit relative to bead N?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived A0 = -A1, which is equivalent to A1 = -A0 as stated in the problem. This is a good moment to affirm their reasoning and immediately push them to apply the same logic to part (b) for the right wall, keeping momentum going.

exchanges.student: i’d add a fake bead \(N+1\) on the right, with the wall halfway between \(N\) and \(N+1\). then i think the same logic gives \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\).
exchanges.tutor: Perfect, that's exactly it. Now for part (c), what do you think the general form of a normal mode solution looks like for a beaded string? Think about what you know about traveling or standing waves on a discrete system.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived the right-wall boundary condition. Now I should push them forward to part (c) — finding the normal modes and frequencies. The key is to connect the boundary conditions to the ansatz for normal modes on a beaded string. I'll ask them to think about what form the normal mode solutions take.

exchanges.student: i’m guessing \(A_j\) should be something like \(Ce^{ikja}\) or maybe a combo like \(A_j=C\sin(kja)+D\cos(kja)\). not sure which form is easiest w these boundary conditions tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct having both options. Try applying the left boundary condition A1 = −A0 to the cosine/sine combo. What does that tell you about D?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea with both forms. I should guide them to think about which form is more natural given the boundary conditions they derived. The sine form naturally satisfies A0 = 0 type conditions. I'll ask them to test which form is more convenient given what they know about A0.

exchanges.student: lemme test it: \(A_j=C\sin(kja)+D\cos(kja)\), so \(A_0=D\) and \(A_1=C\sin ka + D\cos ka\). then \(A_1=-A_0\) gives \(C\sin ka + D(\cos ka+1)=0\), but idk if that means \(D=0\) unless i rewrite the form smarter.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: the boundary condition A0 = −A1 means the solution is *odd* around j = 0. Which of your two terms — sine or cosine — is naturally odd around j = 0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting tangled in algebra. The key insight they're missing is simpler: A0 = D, and from the left boundary condition A1 = -A0, but more directly, if we think of the imaginary bead at position j=0, the condition A0 = -A1 means the solution must be ODD around j=0. An odd function around j=0 means only the sine survives (D=0). I should guide them to think about symmetry rather than algebra.

exchanges.student: right, cosine is even and sine is odd, so i should drop the cosine term. so maybe \(A_j=C\sin(kja)\).
exchanges.tutor: Good. Now apply the right-wall boundary condition A_{N+1} = −A_N to your sine solution. What does that tell you about the allowed values of k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that D=0 and the solution is A_j = C sin(kja). Now I should push them to apply the right-wall boundary condition A_{N+1} = -A_N to find the allowed values of k, which will give the normal mode frequencies.

exchanges.student: i get \( \sin((N+1)ka)=-\sin(Nka)\), but i’m stuck simplifying it tbh. is this where it turns into a half-integer condition on \(k\)?
exchanges.tutor: Good approach. Try rewriting the condition as sin((N+1)ka) + sin(Nka) = 0. Do you know a sum-to-product identity that could simplify the left side?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but stuck on the trigonometry. I should guide them to use a known identity to combine the two sine terms. The key is that sin(X) = -sin(Y) means sin(X) + sin(Y) = 0, which can be handled with sum-to-product. Or more directly, sin(X) = -sin(Y) implies sin(X) = sin(-Y), so X = -Y + 2πn or X = π+Y + 2πn. The useful case here leads to (2N+1)ka = mπ. I'll nudge them toward using the sine identity rather than giving the result.

exchanges.student: yeah i think \( \sin A+\sin B = 2\sin\!\big(\frac{A+B}{2}\big)\cos\!\big(\frac{A-B}{2}\big)\). then that gives \(2\sin\!\big(\frac{(2N+1)ka}{2}\big)\cos\!\big(\frac{ka}{2}\big)=0\), but idk which factor is the physical one to use.
exchanges.tutor: Great algebra. Now think about what each factor being zero implies for k. Are both families of solutions physically distinct and meaningful for a system of N beads? How many independent normal modes do you expect?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly applied the identity and arrived at the right factored form. Now I need to guide them to think about which factor gives physically meaningful solutions. The cos(ka/2)=0 case gives k values that are too large for the discrete system (beyond the Brillouin zone), so the physical solutions come from the sine factor. I'll ask them to think about what each factor implies for k and which ones are physically meaningful.

exchanges.student: i expect \(N\) modes total, so i’m guessing only one branch should survive and give \(N\) allowed \(k\)’s. is the right one \( \sin\!\big(\frac{(2N+1)ka}{2}\big)=0 \), so \(k=\frac{2p\pi}{(2N+1)a}\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that gives N distinct, physical values of k (think about which values of p are independent and non-trivial). Now, do you remember the dispersion relation for the beaded string? Use it to write down the corresponding frequencies.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the right factor and got the allowed k values. I should confirm this and now push them to write down the normal mode frequencies using the dispersion relation for a beaded string, which they should know from earlier in the course.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated excellent Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently guiding the student through each step of Problem 4.1 without giving away answers. The student was led to derive each result independently, with the tutor providing targeted prompts rather than solutions. Scaffolding was well-structured, building progressively from physical intuition to mathematical formalism. Responses were concise and appropriately sized. No off-topic drift, no direct answer-giving, and no rewriting of student work was observed.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted. The tutor never gave a direct answer or near-submission-ready output; every key step (boundary conditions, sine-only form, quantization condition) was elicited from the student via focused questions. Scaffolding built naturally on each correct student response. Responses were bite-sized and interactive throughout. Tone was consistently supportive and formative. The conversation stayed tightly anchored to Problem 4.1 with no drift.
